nativeRange.habitat: Found in slow-moving and still blackwater or clearwater tributaries and flooded forest streams, where it forages along the bottom over soft sandy or muddy substrates among leaf litter and root tangles.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (protects sensitive barbels during foraging)
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
dense planting around tank edges for security

diet.notes: The Spotted Corydoras is a bottom-feeding omnivore that forages along the substrate. Sinking micro-pellets, catfish wafers, and sinking flake form reliable staples, and the species readily accepts live and frozen foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. It should never be expected to survive on leftovers drifting down from other fish; targeted sinking foods are essential to ensure it is adequately fed. In the wild, these fish consume benthic worms, small crustaceans, insect larvae, and organic detritus.

avoidWith: aggressive bottom dwellers competing for territory
any fish large enough to swallow a 6 cm catfish

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase thumbnail; photographer credited as Hippocampus-Bildarchiv. License not explicitly stated on page. Full-resolution image may be available via FishBase photo gallery.

summary: The Spotted Corydoras (Corydoras ambiacus) is a small, peaceful catfish from the Amazon basin of Peru and Ecuador, recognized by its beige-olive body densely covered in small grey-brown spots. Groups of six or more are recommended over fine sand. A 2024 taxonomic revision places the species under the name Brochis ambiacus.
careNotes: The Spotted Corydoras is a generally undemanding community fish well-suited to planted South American biotope setups. Groups of at least six are strongly recommended — solitary or paired individuals tend to be visibly stressed and more prone to disease. A fine sand substrate is the single most important husbandry detail: coarse or sharp gravel abrades the sensitive barbels these fish rely on for foraging, leading to bacterial infections and rapid decline. Maintain a minimum 3 cm sand layer. Filtration should provide gentle flow; sponge filters or spray-bar outputs are ideal. Water quality must be consistent — members of the cory catfish subfamily (Corydoradinae) are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes even when pH and temperature are within range. Regular water changes of 25–30% per week are recommended. The species is frequently mislabelled in the aquarium trade as Corydoras punctatus (reassigned in 2024 to Hoplisoma punctatum) or Corydoras leucomelas (reassigned to Hoplisoma leucomelas). Corydoras ambiacus is distinguishable by its pattern of many small, evenly scattered grey spots over a beige-olive body, with the dorsal fin bearing a dark blotch, and a largely white-tipped leading spine. The 2024 Dias et al. revision reassigns this species to Brochis ambiacus; most hobby sources and the trade still use Corydoras ambiacus, and both names appear in commerce. Like all members of its subfamily, the Spotted Corydoras possesses sharp, lockable pectoral and dorsal spines that can envenomate a handler — use a soft net rather than bare hands or cupped palms. The venom is mildly painful and the wound commonly becomes inflamed; spines can break off in skin. The species is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dart to the surface to gulp atmospheric air, especially in low-oxygen or warm conditions — this is normal behaviour, not a distress indicator, unless the frequency becomes excessive.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable but less reliably triggered than in more commonly bred cory species such as Corydoras aeneus or Corydoras paleatus. Condition a group of at least two females per male on live and frozen foods for several weeks. A substantial cool water change of 20–30%, mimicking the onset of Amazonian seasonal rains, is the standard spawning trigger. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on broad plant leaves, glass, or driftwood surfaces. Remove adults after spawning or transfer eggs to a separate hatching container to prevent predation. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming within another 2–3 days and accept infusoria, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food. Fungused eggs should be removed promptly. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate — the species will spawn given correct conditioning and a trigger, but fry survival rates in community tanks without egg separation are typically low.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras ambiacus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided temperature 21–27°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 36–215 ppm, adult size 60–65 mm SL, native range (Río Ampiyacu, Yavarí, Napo, Nanay, lower Ucayali basins in Peru; Pangayacu and Yasuní systems in Ecuador), diet, behaviour, and note that the species name is commonly misapplied to similar-looking fishes.

additional.name: FishBase — Brochis ambiacus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.9 cm SL), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), native range (upper Amazon basin), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), and facultative air-breathing note. Hardness converted: 2–25 dH ≈ gH 2–25; used gH 2–15 per SeriouslyFish softer-end Amazonian biotope context.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care reference used for tank size (20 gal / ~75 L minimum for standard varieties), substrate guidance, grouping (6+), diet (worms, sinking wafers), and compatibility notes. No C. ambiacus-specific page exists; general genus data applied.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Corydoras revised, new genus names
additional.notes: Used for the 2024 Dias et al. revision details: Corydoras ambiacus reassigned to Brochis ambiacus; C. punctatus to Hoplisoma punctatum; C. leucomelas to Hoplisoma leucomelas. Seven genera: Corydoras, Aspidoras, Scleromystax, Gastrodermus, Osteogaster, Brochis, Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Spotted Cory (Corydoras ambiacus)
additional.notes: Secondary care reference. Confirmed temperature 21–25°C, pH 6.0–7.6, hardness 4–22 dH, adult size 4–5 cm, lifespan 3–5 years, omnivorous diet, peaceful shoaling behaviour, native range (Rio Ambyiacu, Peru). Noted breeding as rare in captivity with cool-water-change trigger.

fish.breedingNotes: Deposits adhesive eggs on plant leaves, glass, and driftwood surfaces. A cool water change is the standard spawning trigger. Adults or eggs should be separated after spawning to protect the fry. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry accept micro-worms and powdered fry food once free-swimming.
